items.front: When is a deviation advisory issued?
items.back: When the aircraft is observed in a position and on a track that will obviously cause it to deviate from protected airspace.

items.front: If necessary, what should ATC do after issuing a deviation advisory?
items.back: Help the aircraft return to the assigned protected airspace.

items.front: What is the total width of an RNAV ATS route described in the note?
items.back: 8 miles.

items.front: How much laterally protected airspace exists on each side of an RNAV ATS route centerline?
items.back: 4 miles on each side.

items.front: What route-centerline performance are aircraft on RNAV ATS routes expected to maintain?
items.back: Within 2 miles of the route centerline.

items.front: What may be indicated by an aircraft approaching the 2-mile RNAV centerline limit?
items.back: A navigation system error or failure.

items.front: When must a fix time be manually recorded?
items.back: At least once for each controlled aircraft in the sector when the EAS FDP flight progress recording components are not operational.

items.front: What type of operation does this manual fix posting rule address?
items.back: En route operations.

items.front: What aircraft does this rule apply to?
items.back: Controlled aircraft in the controller's sector of responsibility.

items.front: What system condition triggers this manual fix posting requirement?
items.back: The EAS FDP flight progress recording components are not operational.

items.front: How often must the observed or reported fix time be manually recorded for each controlled aircraft when this rule is active?
items.back: At least once.

items.front: When may ATC request an estimate or report over a specific fix?
items.back: When necessary.

items.front: After ATC states "radar contact," what happens to compulsory reporting over reporting points?
items.back: It is discontinued.

items.front: When does normal position reporting resume after radar contact has been established?
items.back: When ATC informs the aircraft "radar contact lost" or "radar service terminated."

items.front: Name one published position-report request from this paragraph.
items.back: Over/passing (fix), miles from (fix), miles direction of (fix/airway/location), crossing/joining/departing (airway or route), or intercepting/crossing a named NAVAID radial.

items.front: Does radar contact permanently bar the controller from requesting a specific fix report?
items.back: No.

items.front: What statement from ATC causes compulsory position reporting to stop?
items.back: "Radar contact."

items.front: What must generally be done when radar service is terminated?
items.back: Inform the aircraft that radar service is terminated.

items.front: What phraseology is used to terminate radar service?
items.back: Radar service terminated. Add nonradar routing if required.

items.front: Name one situation in which radar service is automatically terminated and the aircraft need not be separately advised.
items.back: Cancellation of IFR outside the listed radar-service areas, or an aircraft on an instrument/visual/contact approach has landed or has been instructed to change to advisory frequency.

items.front: At a tower-controlled airport where radar coverage does not exist to within one-half mile of the runway end, what must be done for arriving aircraft?
items.back: They must be informed when radar service is terminated.

items.front: Does IFR cancellation inside Class B, Class C, TRSA, or where basic radar service is provided automatically terminate radar service without further advisory?
items.back: No.

items.front: If an aircraft has been instructed to change to advisory frequency while conducting an instrument, visual, or contact approach, what happens to radar service?
items.back: It is automatically terminated.
